_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Dane County, WI. Record survival, courthouse history, and the consequences of the 1904 capitol fire for state-level records. County records are generally excellent; the fire destroyed the state capitol and some state-level records but did not affect the county courthouse.

current_courthouse.location: Madison, WI — 215 S. Hamilton Street (City-County Building)

previous_courthouses.fate: Early frame and brick courthouses. Replaced as the county grew. No significant fire loss to county records.
Served until replaced by the City-County Building. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: Wisconsin State Capitol fire — this destroyed STATE records, not county records. The county courthouse was a separate building.
records_destroyed: Some state-level records housed in the capitol were lost, including legislative papers and some executive department files
records_survived: All county-level records survived — the county courthouse was not the capitol
record_losses.scope: State records only — no impact on county genealogical records
record_losses.description: The 1904 fire destroyed the state capitol building, not the Dane County courthouse. This is a common confusion: the capitol and the courthouse were separate buildings. County deed books, probate files, court records, and vital records were not affected. Some state-level records (legislative journals, executive correspondence) were lost, but genealogically significant county records are intact from county formation (1839).
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1904 capitol fire is famous but did NOT destroy Dane County's genealogical records. If {ancestor_name} lived in Dane County, the deeds, wills, and court records survive. Look for gaps in state-level records (military muster rolls, some territorial papers) but expect county records to be complete.
record_losses.source: Wisconsin Historical Society. Guide to Wisconsin County Records. Madison: WHS, various editions.

deeds.repository: Dane County Register of Deeds; Wisconsin Historical Society microfilm

wills_estates.completeness: Good — probate files from near county formation
wills_estates.gaps: Minor gaps in earliest loose papers. Bound volumes intact.
wills_estates.repository: Dane County Probate Court; Wisconsin Historical Society

marriages.completeness: Good — Wisconsin required marriage licenses from territorial period
marriages.gaps: Some early territorial records fragmentary. Post-1850 essentially complete.
marriages.repository: Dane County Register of Deeds (marriage licenses); Wisconsin Historical Society

court_minutes.completeness: Good — circuit court records from county formation
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in territorial-era justice of the peace records
court_minutes.repository: Dane County Clerk of Courts; Wisconsin Historical Society

vital_records.completeness: Wisconsin required statewide vital registration from 1907. Pre-1907, church records and census are primary sources. Madison city health records may predate 1907.
vital_records.repository: Wisconsin Department of Health Services; Wisconsin Historical Society for historical records

substitute_sources.description: UW Madison student records, faculty records, and institutional files document thousands of Dane County residents. The university was established in 1848 and maintained detailed student registers, grade records, and housing records.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Student registers include hometown, parent names, and academic records. Valuable for tracking families with university connections.

substitute_sources.description: Norwegian Lutheran congregations in western Dane County maintained detailed ministerialbok (parish registers) including baptisms, confirmations, marriages, and burials. Records were kept in Norwegian until the 1920s-1930s.
substitute_sources.repository: Norwegian-American Historical Association, St. Olaf College; Luther Seminary Archives; individual congregations; Wisconsin Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Norwegian families. Ministerialbok records often include parents' home parish in Norway, enabling research to link American families to Norwegian origins.
substitute_sources.source: Qualey, Carlton C. Norwegian Settlement in the United States. Northfield, MN: Norwegian-American Historical Association, 1938.

substitute_sources.description: Madison being the state capital, many Dane County residents worked for state government. Personnel files, payroll records, and appointment records document state employees.
substitute_sources.repository: Wisconsin Historical Society; Wisconsin Department of Administration
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Useful for identifying government employees. Civil service records include names, addresses, salaries, and dates of service.

narrative_hooks: Dane County's records are essentially complete from 1839 — the 1904 fire destroyed the state capitol, not the county courthouse, and {ancestor_name}'s deeds, wills, and court records survived.

If {ancestor_name} was Norwegian and settled in western Dane County, the church ministerialbok may contain more detail than any county record — baptisms, confirmations, and the home parish in Norway that connects the American family to the old country.
Wisconsin required vital registration from 1907 — before that date, {ancestor_name}'s birth, marriage, or death must be traced through church records, census, or newspaper notices.

Madison was a capital, a college town, and a county seat — {ancestor_name} may appear in university records, state government files, or county documents, depending on which version of Madison they inhabited.
source_references: Wisconsin Historical Society. Guide to Wisconsin County Records. Madison: WHS, various editions.
Qualey, Carlton C. Norwegian Settlement in the United States. Northfield, MN: Norwegian-American Historical Association, 1938.
Mollenhoff, David V. Madison: A History of the Formative Years. 2nd ed. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 2003.
